medications.indication_rationale: Robust oral anti-inflammatory analgesia for the acute inflammatory flare, appropriate as a symptomatic bridge WHILE arranging urgent in-person assessment — it must not substitute for excluding septic arthritis, and it may partially mask fever, so escalation advice still applies.
medications.dose_route_frequency: naproxen 500 mg orally twice daily with food (as stated in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution with mildly elevated blood pressure (140/85); review renal function, GI ulcer risk and cardiovascular risk before continuing

medications.indication_rationale: Adjunct/alternative analgesia if NSAID is contraindicated or insufficient; does not treat inflammation but is a safe additional analgesic.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1 g orally up to four times daily (maximum 4 g/day)

medications.indication_rationale: Blind oral antibiotics for a possible septic joint are an error of commission — they do not treat a septic joint adequately, delay definitive aspiration, and render synovial-fluid culture uninterpretable. If septic arthritis is a concern the correct action is urgent in-person aspiration, not remote antibiotics.

This is not confirmed gout (first-MTP spared, atypical erythema) and urate-lowering therapy must never be started during an acute attack; starting anti-gout therapy remotely would be an error of commission.

Initiation of a DMARD to reduce future palindromic attacks is a planned GP/rheumatology decision at follow-up (noted in the source care plan) — it is NOT an acute telehealth action and is not started during this encounter.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest the joint and apply an ice pack wrapped in a towel for 15 minutes every 2 hours, keeping the foot elevated above the level of the heart (RICE).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces local inflammation, swelling and pain as a symptomatic bridge to the urgent in-person assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not attempt to weight-bear or walk normally on the foot; use assisted mobilisation (crutches if available) and clear a safe pathway to reduce falls risk.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient is non-weight-bearing and hopping with a stated falls risk; safe mobility prevents a secondary injury.

patient_education_points.point: An acute hot, swollen, very painful joint needs to be examined in person because a joint infection cannot be ruled out over a phone or video call — this is why same-day face-to-face assessment is important even if you feel otherwise well.
Not having a fever does NOT rule out a joint infection — go by the joint, not the temperature.
If this turns out to be an inflammatory flare, these attacks are intense but usually settle within 48-72 hours and do not cause lasting joint damage; anti-inflammatories and ice help the symptoms.
Follow up with your GP to reassess and discuss whether preventive medication is appropriate to reduce future attacks.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment tonight/within 24 hours to exclude septic arthritis; then GP review at 48-72 hours to confirm resolution of the flare and discuss preventive treatment.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Whether septic arthritis has been excluded (examination +/- aspiration), resolution or progression of the flare, analgesia adequacy, and mobility/falls safety.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Fever, rigors, spreading redness, the joint becoming agonising to light touch, feeling systemically unwell, or inability to obtain a same-day in-person review — present to the emergency department.

must_include_items: Recognise this as an acute monoarthritis (acute hot swollen single joint)
State that septic arthritis cannot be excluded via telehealth and must be assessed in person
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / GP able to aspirate, or ED after hours)
Provide clear safety-netting for fever, rigors, spreading redness or systemic illness -> ED/000
Offer symptomatic relief (NSAID/analgesia + RICE) as a bridge, not as definitive management
Address the falls risk from non-weight-bearing mobility
acceptable_alternatives: Direct ED referral instead of urgent same-day GP is acceptable (arguably safer) given the acute hot joint
Paracetamol in place of/alongside the NSAID where the NSAID is contraindicated
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange urgent in-person assessment
Failing to safety-net the septic-arthritis red flags
errors_of_commission: Confidently diagnosing a benign inflammatory flare remotely and advising home management only
Using the absence of fever to exclude septic arthritis
Starting empirical antibiotics remotely for a possible septic joint
Diagnosing gout and starting colchicine/allopurinol remotely
Initiating a DMARD or oral corticosteroid remotely
Discharging with reassurance and no in-person review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) arranging urgent in-person assessment because septic arthritis cannot be excluded remotely, and (b) safety-netting the septic/systemic red flags. Remote home-management-only of the acute hot joint is a critical failure regardless of other content.
